title: The Update Gate
concept: The update gate in a GRU acts like the forget and input gates combined, deciding how much of the past state to keep versus how much new content to write.

exercises.prompt: What does the update gate in a GRU decide?

options.text: How much past memory to keep versus new content to write.
How to completely erase the memory of the network.
Whether to stop the model from running entirely.

exercises.explain: The update gate decides how much past information to keep versus how much new candidate information to write.

cards.title: Two Jobs, One Gate
cards.body: In older AI models, remembering the past and learning new things were handled by two separate managers. GRUs make this simpler: they use just one manager, the update gate, to balance the old and the new at the same time!

cards.title: What is the Update Gate?
cards.body: In a GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit), the update gate ($z_t$) is a single controller that decides how much of the previous memory to keep and how much new information to mix in. It does the job of both the 'forget gate' and the 'input gate' found in older networks like LSTMs.

cards.title: The Recipe Slider
cards.body: Imagine you are rewriting a recipe. Instead of copying the old recipe to a new page and then erasing parts of it, you use a slider. If you slide it to the left, you keep the old recipe exactly as it was. If you slide it to the right, you replace it entirely with a new cooking style. The update gate is that single slider!

cards.title: Tracking a Story
cards.body: When reading a sentence like 'Alice went to the store, and then she bought an apple,' the update gate decides to keep the subject 'Alice' in memory (keeping the past) while updating the action from 'went to the store' to 'bought an apple' (adding new content).

cards.title: Do We Need Two Gates?
cards.body: People often think a GRU needs separate gates to forget and to remember. In reality, the update gate does both! If it decides to keep 80% of the old memory, it automatically limits the new information to just 20%. It is a perfect balance.

cards.title: The Memory Balance
cards.body: See how the update gate shifts the focus between what we already know and what we are currently seeing.

right.text: When the gate is close to 0, we overwrite with the latest information.

left.text: When the gate is close to 1, we preserve our long-term memory.

exercises.prompt: Why is the GRU's update gate considered efficient?

options.text: It combines the forget and input decisions into one gate.
It requires twice as many calculations as an LSTM.
It only lets new data in without looking at the past.

exercises.explain: Unlike LSTMs which use separate gates for input and forgetting, the GRU's update gate combines these two roles into one.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The update gate in a GRU acts like the forget and input gates combined.
exercises.explain: True! The update gate decides both how much of the old state to keep (forgetting) and how much new candidate state to add (inputting).

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe the primary function of the update gate.
exercises.sentence: The update gate is used to balance the past memory and new information.
exercises.explain: The update gate acts as a single slider to balance how much of the old history we keep versus how much new content we write.

exercises.prompt: How does the AI determine the value of the update gate?

options.text: The AI flips a virtual coin to decide.
The AI calculates a balance between 0 and 1 based on current and past data.
The AI randomly deletes half of the words.

exercises.explain: AI models do not randomly guess. The update gate uses a mathematical calculation (a sigmoid function) to output a precise balance between 0 and 1.

exercises.prompt: You are building a GRU model to summarize books. When reading a new chapter, how does the update gate decide what to do?

options.text: It resets the entire network memory to zero.
It only lets new information through and ignores the past.
It balances keeping past memory versus writing new candidate information.
It multiplies the past and the new information together.

exercises.explain: The update gate controls both remembering and writing. If it outputs 0.9, it keeps 90% of the past (remembering) and only adds 10% of the new candidate state (updating). If it outputs 0.1, it keeps only 10% of the past and writes 90% of the new information.
